questions.scenario: You are presenting to senior leadership and suddenly notice your voice is shaky and your palms are sweating. You have five minutes left.

options.text: Push through and ignore the physical sensations
Acknowledge internally that you are anxious and focus on your breathing
Apologize for being nervous to the audience
Speed up to finish faster and escape the situation

questions.scenario: Your manager gives you vague feedback: 'You need to be more strategic.' You feel confused and defensive but are not sure why.

options.text: Ask for examples immediately to prove them wrong
Notice your defensiveness and explore what 'strategic' means to you
Agree vaguely and hope the issue goes away
Research what strategic thinking means before responding

questions.scenario: You have been passed over for a promotion you expected. You tell everyone you are fine, but you find yourself avoiding the person who got the role.

options.text: Keep telling yourself you are fine and act professionally
Admit to yourself that you feel disappointment and possibly envy
Confront your manager about the unfair decision
Ask the promoted colleague how they prepared for the role

questions.scenario: You are working on a project you initially loved, but now dread opening your laptop each morning. You cannot pinpoint what changed.

options.text: Push through assuming it is just a phase
Journal about when the dread started and what shifted
Blame external factors like a difficult client
Schedule a vacation to reset your energy

questions.scenario: During a performance review, you realize you have been interrupting your manager when they give critical feedback.

options.text: Continue interrupting to clarify your perspective
Notice the pattern and consciously listen without interrupting
Shut down and stop participating in the review
Ask if you can respond after they finish each point

questions.scenario: You feel a surge of excitement about a new idea in a meeting, but notice your teammates seem skeptical.

options.text: Push harder to convince them with more enthusiasm
Pause to notice if your excitement is masking uncertainty
Abandon the idea since they do not share your excitement
Ask what concerns they have about the idea

questions.scenario: You receive an email at 11 PM from your boss asking for changes to a presentation by 8 AM. You feel immediate rage.

options.text: Reply immediately expressing how unreasonable this is
Wait until morning, then assess if the request is truly urgent
Do the work resentfully while complaining to a colleague
Set boundaries about after-hours requests in your reply

questions.scenario: A colleague takes credit for your idea in a meeting with executives. You feel your face flush with anger.

options.text: Interrupt immediately to correct the record
Take a breath and decide the best moment to clarify
Say nothing but harbor resentment toward them
Privately message your manager to set the record straight

questions.scenario: Your project deadline is moved up by two weeks. You feel panic and want to tell your team it is impossible.

options.text: Immediately tell leadership the deadline is impossible
Regulate your panic and create options before responding
Accept the deadline and work yourself to exhaustion
Ask what can be deprioritized to meet the new date

questions.scenario: You are in a high-stakes negotiation and the other party just made a personal comment about your experience level.

options.text: Respond with a personal comment about their track record
Notice your triggered feeling and refocus on the business issue
Shut down and let your colleague take over the negotiation
Call a break to collect yourself before continuing

questions.scenario: You have been working 60-hour weeks for a month. A minor technical glitch makes you want to throw your laptop.

options.text: Express your frustration loudly so others know your stress
Recognize your exhaustion and take a 10-minute walk
Power through and fix the glitch despite your frustration
Complain to a colleague about your workload

questions.scenario: Your peer wins an award you were also nominated for. You feel a mix of happiness for them and disappointment for yourself.

options.text: Avoid the celebration event to process your feelings
Attend and authentically celebrate while acknowledging your own disappointment
Attend but make comments about how the selection process works
Send a congratulatory message but skip the event

questions.scenario: A usually talkative direct report has been silent in your last three one-on-ones, giving short answers.

options.text: Assume they are just busy and stick to your agenda
Ask directly if something is affecting their engagement
Tell them they need to be more participative in meetings
Ask other team members if they know what is going on

questions.scenario: A client is being unusually difficult in a meeting, making demands that seem irrational. You notice their voice is strained.

options.text: Push back firmly on their unreasonable demands
Acknowledge they seem under pressure and ask what is driving the urgency
Give in to their demands to end the uncomfortable meeting
Suggest rescheduling when they have had time to prepare

questions.scenario: Your colleague is presenting and you notice their hands are shaking and they keep losing their place.

options.text: Do nothing - it is not your place to intervene
Ask a clarifying question to give them a moment to regroup
Take over the presentation to help them out
Tell them afterwards they seemed nervous

questions.scenario: A team member from a different cultural background seems offended by something you said, but you are not sure what.

options.text: Assume they are too sensitive and move on
Ask if you said something that landed differently than intended
Avoid them to prevent further miscommunication
Research their culture later to understand what happened

questions.scenario: Your manager seems unusually short-tempered in a meeting, snapping at small issues that normally would not bother them.

options.text: Assume they are a bad manager and document the behavior
Consider they might be under pressure and check in privately
Ignore it and avoid them until they normalize
Ask colleagues if they know what is going on with the manager

questions.scenario: A junior employee shares an idea in a meeting that you tried two years ago and know will not work.

options.text: Immediately explain why it will not work based on your experience
Ask questions to understand their thinking before sharing your experience
Stay silent and let them learn from their mistake
Privately tell them later about your previous attempt

questions.scenario: Two team members are in open conflict during a project meeting, disagreeing on approach and raising voices.

options.text: Let them work it out while you continue the meeting
Acknowledge the tension and suggest a structured problem-solving approach
Take sides with the person you agree with
Schedule a separate meeting for them to resolve this

questions.scenario: You need to give critical feedback to a high-performing employee who has been missing deadlines recently.

options.text: Send an email to document the issue formally
Have a private conversation focused on impact and support
Mention it casually in a team meeting so they get the message
Wait to see if the pattern continues before saying anything

questions.scenario: You are new to a team and need to build relationships quickly to be effective in your role.

options.text: Focus on your work output first - relationships will follow
Schedule brief one-on-ones to learn about each colleague's role and goals
Join group lunches and dominate the conversation to show your expertise
Wait for others to reach out and welcome you

questions.scenario: Your team needs to make a decision where you have strong expertise, but others have different opinions based on gut feeling.

options.text: Insist on your approach since you have the data to back it up
Share your data while validating their perspectives and finding common ground
Let them decide and prepare to say I told you so later
Propose a pilot test to compare approaches

questions.scenario: You need to decline a request from a senior leader that would derail your team's priorities.

options.text: Say yes and figure out how to manage the overload later
Explain your current priorities and ask what should be deprioritized
Simply say no and cite your full workload
Delegate the request to a team member without explaining why

questions.scenario: A cross-functional project is failing because departments are not communicating. You are not the official lead.

options.text: Document the communication failures and escalate to leadership
Convene an informal session to align on goals and establish communication rhythms
Focus only on your part and let the official lead handle it
Publicly call out the communication gaps in the next meeting
